source_texts: That comes, I think, from their tiredness. Over the centuries they have taken upon themselves the entire task of love; they have always played the whole dialogue—both parts. For man has only repeated their words, and done it badly. And has made their learning difficult with his distractedness, his negligence, his jealousy, which was itself a kind of negligence.
“Where to?” Alyosha whispered back, glancing around him. They were alone in a smallish garden. The house was at least fifty yards away. “Why do you have to whisper?”
“Thandavaraya, I don’t think this is a real horse. It may belong to the poi kaal kudirai (false horse performance) group,” said one of the soldiers. “Fine then.

As for the ship’s crew, they longed with all their hearts to meet the unicorn and so harpoon it, hoist it on board, and carve it up. They painstakingly scrutinized the sea. Captain Farragut had in fact promised $2,000 to the first person to spot the animal, whether cabin boy, able seaman, mate, or officer. So I leave to your imagination how much those on board the Abraham Lincoln used their eyes.
Now these men, or these women, who live behind these four walls, they dress in rough homespun, they are equals, they call each other brothers. That is all very well. But do they do anything else?

Again he produced the whole wad of bills, counted off three of them, tossed them onto the counter, and hurriedly left the store, accompanied by the shop assistants and errand boys, all of them bowing to him and wishing him a good time. Andrei cleared his throat after swallowing his brandy and jumped up onto the driver’s box. But just as Mitya was installing himself in the cart, to his great surprise, Fenya appeared. She was running, completely out of breath, and as soon as she reached him, she let out a cry and threw herself down at his feet.
The ‘principal tenant’ of Jean Valjean’s day had died and had been replaced by another exactly like her. I am not sure which philosopher said, ‘There is never any shortage of old women.’
"What is there to say concerning love?" at length said Bazarov. "In passing, you mentioned a certain Madame Odintsov (I think that was the name?). Who is she?"
‘How can you doubt it! But you, who do not love her, why are you so ready to endanger your life? It must be that you hate Erik.’

We started laughing and joking, we talked without stopping the whole time, I didn't know what I was saying, I was happy. She told me she had seen me one time earlier, a long time ago, in the theater. I was with three friends and had behaved like a lunatic; no question, I had been drunk that time too, that was naughty.
rationales: Outputs 2 and 4 exhibit a unique and shared structural fingerprint. Both paraphrase the source's opening idea ("It comes, I believe, of their weariness") but place it differently. In output 2, it's the first sentence. In output 4, an almost identical phrase ("That comes, I think, from their weariness") is moved to the very end of the text. This kind of major structural inversion is a distinctive habit. Additionally, both outputs 2 and 4 uniquely use the singular possessive pronoun "his" to refer to man ("his inattention, his neglect", "his distraction, his carelessness"), whereas the other outputs use the plural "their". Finally, the clause explaining jealousy is structured identically in both: "...his jealousy, which was itself a form of neglect" (2) and "...his jealousy, which was itself a kind of carelessness" (4). These shared stylistic and structural choices strongly suggest they originate from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 exhibit a unique and identical structural change to the source text that is not present in the other options. Both of them invent and prepend a new, opening question about direction or destination ("Where are we going?" in 3, and "Where are you going?" in 4). Following this invented question, both then include the original question about whispering ("Why are you speaking so quietly?" in 3 and "Why do you whisper?" in 4). This specific pattern of adding a question about movement before the question about whispering is a strong tell-tale sign of the same underlying paraphrasing logic. The minor differences in word choice (e.g., "asked softly" vs. "whispered", "quite a distance away" vs. "fifty yards away") are likely stochastic variations from the same model, while the shared structural fingerprint is the key evidence.

Outputs 1 and 2 share a distinct paraphrasing strategy that sets them apart from the others. Both of them convert the original direct quote into indirect speech, starting with the phrase "One of the soldiers..." and using a verb of doubt ("expressed doubts" vs. "doubted"). Furthermore, instead of using the specific cultural term "poi kaal kudirai" (which outputs 0, 3, and 4 all reference in some form), both 1 and 2 opt to describe the performance in generic terms ("a troupe of performers who pretend to mount and ride a still object" and "a group that staged performances with fake horses"). This shared structural approach—shifting to indirect speech and providing a functional description rather than a name—is a strong indicator that they were produced by the same system.

Outputs 1 and 3 share several unique and identical phrases, which is a strong indicator that they were produced by the same system. Specifically, both texts contain the exact phrases "harpoon it, bring it aboard, and butcher it" and "They meticulously scanned the ocean." No other pair of outputs shares this level of verbatim overlap. While there are minor differences, such as output 3's grammatical error ("incentivize" used as a noun), these identical, non-trivial rephrasings serve as a clear linguistic fingerprint of a single paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 1 share a distinct and slightly unnatural core structure that is not present in the other options. Both begin by identifying the inhabitants as "these men, or these women" and specify their location as being "behind these four walls." They then follow a similar checklist of observations: their clothing, their status as "equals," and the fact they "call each other brothers." While there are minor differences in wording (e.g., "coarse woollen" vs. "coarse homespun") and grammar (Output 0 uses the awkward question "but they do something else?" while Output 1 uses the more standard "But do they do anything else?"), the fundamental building blocks and the sequence of ideas are nearly identical, suggesting they were produced by the same system with slight variations in its output.

Outputs 1 and 4 share several distinct and unusual linguistic fingerprints, suggesting they come from the same source. The most compelling piece of evidence is the choice of the word "box" for the driver's seat. Output 1 uses "driver's box" and Output 4 uses "the box." No other output uses this specific, slightly archaic term, with others opting for "driving seat" or "carriage seat." Additionally, both outputs introduce Fenya's arrival using a very similar sentence structure starting with "But..." and containing the phrase "to his surprise" (1: "But just when Mitya was getting into the cart, to his great surprise, Fenya appeared."; 4: "But Mitya was only just taking his seat when suddenly to his surprise he saw Fenya before him."). Finally, both connect Andrey's state to the brandy he drank in a similar way (1: "swallowed his brandy, and jumped up"; 4: "coughing from the brandy he had just swallowed, jumped up"). While there are differences, such as the overall ordering of events and the choice between "threw herself" and "plumped down," the collection of shared, specific stylistic choices points strongly to a single origin.

Outputs 3 and 4 share a key linguistic fingerprint that strongly suggests they were produced by the same system. Both outputs use the exact same, specific phrasing for the philosopher's quote: '“There's always an abundance of elderly women.”' While other outputs provide different versions of this quote, the identical wording here is a significant clue. Additionally, the sentences leading up to the quote are structurally and tonally similar, using informal, first-person expressions of uncertainty: 'I can't recall which philosopher noted,' (in 3) and 'I'm unsure which philosopher commented,' (in 4). The variation in the first part of the sentence ('The individual renting...' vs. 'The woman who was the primary resident...') is typical of a paraphrasing model trying to add variety, but the shared patterns in the latter half of the text reveal the common origin.

Outputs 3 and 4 share a very distinctive and identical linguistic fingerprint. Both of them paraphrase the uncertainty about the name ("I think that's the name") by using the exact same tag question: "I believe?".

- Output 3: "...you brought up a Madame Odintsov, I believe?"
- Output 4: "...someone named Madame Odintsov, I believe?"

While other outputs express this uncertainty in different ways (e.g., Output 0 uses parentheses "(I think that's the name)" and Output 2 uses a full sentence "That was what you called her, I think?"), the use of the specific, slightly formal tag question "I believe?" is unique to and identical in outputs 3 and 4. This points to a shared stylistic preference of the underlying paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 3 share several distinctive linguistic fingerprints. Most notably, they are the only two outputs to use the specific phrase "affection for her" ("hold no affection for her" in 0, and "doesn't have affection for her" in 3). Others use phrases like "do not love her" or "don't even have feelings for her". Additionally, both outputs introduce their final conclusion with a similar speculative structure: "It seems your true motive must be..." (0) and "It's as if you must be..." (3). This contrasts with the more direct conclusions in the other outputs. Finally, both opt for more formal and descriptive phrases for the concept of hatred ("a profound hatred for Erik" in 0, and "consumed by antagonism towards Erik" in 3), rather than the simpler "hate Erik" seen in other versions. These combined similarities in vocabulary, sentence structure, and tone strongly suggest a common origin.

Outputs 1 and 4 are strikingly similar in their structure and phrasing, strongly suggesting they were generated by the same system. 

1. **Identical Structure:** Both 1 and 4 reverse the order of events compared to the other outputs. They both begin with the woman's story about seeing the narrator at the theater and then conclude with the narrator's reaction of laughing and talking. The other outputs (0, 2, 3) all start with the laughing and talking first. This shared, unique structural choice is a major fingerprint.

2. **Shared Unique Phrases:** Both outputs use the distinctive phrase "no question" when describing the narrator's past intoxication. This exact phrase does not appear in any other output.

3. **Parallel Phrasing and Vocabulary:** The sentence constructions are nearly identical. 
   - Output 1: "...behaved like a lunatic; there is no question, I had been drunk then, too, which was mean."
   - Output 4: "...behaved like a crazy man; no question, I had been drunk that time, too, that was bad."
   The use of a near-synonym ("lunatic" vs. "crazy man") and a similarly simplistic, judgmental final clause ("which was mean" vs. "that was bad") points to the same underlying template. 

4. **Overall Flow:** The final sentences describing the laughter are also constructed in a parallel manner: 
   - Output 1: "We began to laugh and joke, we talked the whole time without stopping..."
   - Output 4: "We started laughing and joking, we talked without stopping the whole time..."

These multiple layers of similarity, from the high-level structure down to specific, idiosyncratic phrases, make a compelling case that outputs 1 and 4 originated from the same paraphraser.

human_par3: It comes, I believe, of their weariness. For centuries they have performed all of love; they have always played the entire dialogue, both parts. For man merely repeated what they said, and did it badly. And made it difficult for them to learn, with his inattention, his neglect, his jealousy, which was itself a form of neglect.

This is because they are weary, I think. For centuries they have been responsible for performing everything that love entails, they have always played both parts, speaking both sides of the dialogue. Men have only ever repeated their lines, badly, complicating women’s study of their roles with their abstraction, their thoughtlessness and their jealousy, which is itself a form of thoughtlessness.
llama_31_8b: It's likely due to their exhaustion, as they've carried the weight of love for centuries, taking on both roles in the relationship. Meanwhile, men have simply echoed their words, often poorly, and complicated their efforts with their own carelessness, jealousy, and lack of attention.
gemini_25_flash: I believe their exhaustion is the root cause. Throughout history, they've shouldered the entire burden of love, carrying both sides of the conversation. Men have merely echoed their words, and poorly at that. And through their inattentiveness, carelessness, and even their jealousies (which were a form of neglect), men have made women's lessons in love more challenging.
dipper_lowmed: Over the centuries they have taken on themselves the whole task of love, they have always played the whole dialogue, both parts, for man has only repeated their words, and done it badly. And has made their learning difficult by his distraction, his carelessness, his jealousy, which was itself a kind of carelessness. That comes, I think, from their weariness.

And through their inattentiveness, carelessness, and even their jealousies (which were a form of neglect), men have made women's lessons in love more challenging.
"Where are we going?" Alyosha asked softly, looking around the small garden. The house was quite a distance away. "Why are you speaking so quietly?"
“Thandavaraya, you'd better make sure this animal really is a horse, and not a false-legged prop, brought for the temple festivities,” mocked another soldier.

The ship's crew eagerly anticipated encountering the unicorn, hoping to harpoon it, bring it aboard, and butcher it. They meticulously scanned the ocean, driven by Captain Farragut's promise of a $2,000 reward to anyone who spotted the creature, regardless of rank. This incentivize caused them to be extremely watchful.

These individuals, living within the confines of these four walls, wear simple, coarse clothing and treat each other as equals, referring to one another as brothers. While this is commendable, one might ask if their activities extend beyond this.

The man then paid the store clerk and handed over three bills, before hastily departing the store as the clerk and delivery boys bid him farewell. Andrei took a sip of brandy, cleared his throat, and climbed onto the carriage seat. Just as Mitya settled in, Fenya suddenly appeared, panting heavily, and rushed to Mitya's side. She burst into a cry and collapsed at his feet.
The individual renting from Jean Valjean had passed away, only to be succeeded by someone remarkably similar. I can't recall which philosopher noted, "There's always an abundance of elderly women."
Eventually, Bazarov spoke up, asking, "Regarding love, what can be said? Earlier, you brought up a Madame Odintsov, I believe? Could you tell me more about her?"
You have so much doubt, but as one who doesn't have affection for her, why would you be willing to risk your life? It's as if you must be consumed by antagonism towards Erik.

We started laughing and joking and barely stopped for a second, my words tumbling out without a pause. I was completely carefree. One of the things she mentioned was that she had seen me before, long ago, at the theater. Apparently, I was with a group of friends at the time and got quite carried away, even by my own standards - I had indeed been drinking.

Over the centuries they have taken on themselves the whole task of love, they have always played the whole dialogue, both parts, for man has only repeated their words, and done it badly. And has made their learning difficult by his distraction, his carelessness, his jealousy, which was itself a kind of carelessness. That comes, I think, from their weariness.
"Where are you going?" Alyosha whispered. "Why do you whisper?" He looked round him. They were alone in the garden; the house was at least fifty yards away.
"Thandavaraya," a soldier remarked, "I doubt this is an actual horse. It's likely from a 'false horse' dance troupe." "Very well," came the reply.

The crew of the ship were eager to catch sight of the unicorn, with the intention of capturing and killing it. The captain had offered a significant reward of $2,000 to anyone who could spot the creature, leading the crew to keep a particularly sharp lookout for it.
They dressed in rough homespun, they called themselves brothers. All that was very good; but did they do anything else?

He pulled the bundle of notes out of his pocket again, picked out three hundred roubles, threw them on the counter, and ran hurriedly out of the shop. Every one followed him out, bowing and wishing him good luck. Andrey, coughing from the brandy he had just swallowed, jumped up on the box. But Mitya was only just taking his seat when suddenly to his surprise he saw Fenya before him. She ran up panting, clasped her hands before him with a cry, and plumped down at his feet.

The woman who was the primary resident in Jean Valjean's life passed away, and another woman, her spitting image, took her place. I'm unsure which philosopher commented, "There's always an abundance of elderly women."
"So, about love?" Bazarov finally spoke. "You briefly mentioned someone named Madame Odintsov, I believe? Who is that?"
"You're blind to the situation! What drives you to take such a reckless risk when you don't even have feelings for her? It must be that your actions are actually rooted in hatred for Erik."

She told me that she had seen me once, long ago, at the theatre. I was with three friends, and I behaved like a crazy man; no question, I had been drunk that time, too, that was bad. We started laughing and joking, we talked without stopping the whole time, I did not know what I was saying, I was so happy.
